coredata.dc:title: A strategy for the preparation of low dielectric FGQD/PSPI composite films in wafer-level packaging

Zhang, Jialin

Sun, Rong

Photosensitive polyimide
Fluorinated graphene quantum dots
Wafer-level packaging

XPS and heat resistance of FGQD
Lithography process and performance evaluation of FGQD/PSPI

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of PAE synthesis; (b) FGQD/PSPI lithography process; (c) UV curing process.
(a) NMR spectrum of PAE, (b) molecular weight spectrum of PAE.
(a) XPS spectra of FGQD, (b) thermal weight loss spectra of FGQD.
FTIR spectra of FGQD/PSPI composite films.
FIB cross-section of FGQD/PSPI composite films: (a)Pure-PSPI, (b) PSPI-0.2, (c) PSPI-0.5, (d) PSPI-1, (e)Pure-PSPI, (f) PSPI-0.2, (g) PSPI-0.5, (h) PSPI-1.
Dielectric property of FGQD/PSPI composite films: (a) dielectric constants, (b) dielectric loss.

The image of specimen: (a) the specimen before testing, (b) the specimen that was pulled apart after testing; and the mechanical properties of FGQD/PSPI composite films: (c) Stress, (d) Elongation at break, (e) Tensile strength, (f) Modulus.
Thermal property of FGQD/PSPI composite films: (a) T g curve, (b) T g, (c) T 5 % curve, (d) T 5 %, (e) CTE partial curve, (f) CTE.
Water contact angle of FGQD/PSPI composite films.

head.ce:title: A strategy for the preparation of low dielectric FGQD/PSPI composite films in wafer-level packaging

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Polyimide (PI) exhibited commendable physical and chemical attributes, including notable thermal stability, mechanical properties, processability, and flame resistance [1–6] . It has garnered significant interest for applications in solar cells [7–9] , organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) [10–12], flexible printed circuits [13–15], liquid crystal displays [16–18], aerospace technologies [19–21], and advanced packaging [22–24]. With the emerging Information and Communication Technology (ICT) innovations, exemplified by 5 G, a rapid dissemination of signals is highly needed, but characterized by inadequate penetration and heightened attenuation. The signal crosstalk, loss, and delay were arising from an augmented number of Input/Output (I/O) connections and diminished chip dimensions. In order to address this issue, the development of polyimide materials featuring low dielectric constants and dielectric losses becomes essential [25,26].

According to the Clausius-Mossotti equation, the reduction of dielectric constant can be accomplished by incorporating large-volume rigid side groups, intrinsic pore fillers, or forming mesoporous structures and pores to increase free volume [27–30]. Due to the low dielectric constant of air (≈1), encapsulating air in pores at a nanoscale can significantly reduce the dielectric constant of PI [31]. For example, Li [32] and colleagues successfully constructed porous structures through phase separation, and synthesized a series of porous copolyimide (co-PI) membranes containing trifluoromethyl (CF3) groups. The porous structure allowed for the integration of low dielectric constant air into the polymer matrix, resulting in an exceptionally low dielectric constant ranging from 1.70 to 2.10 at 1 MHz. However, excessive pore structure is not conducive to reducing water absorption rate. Introduction of functional groups containing fluorine, lipid-bond units, fluorinated fillers, or blending with low-polarized inorganic fillers are commonly used approaches to decrease the molar polarizability and subsequently reduce the dielectric constant [33–35]. Designing the molecular chain structure of polyimide (PI) to enhance its dielectric and other comprehensive properties is effective, but the synthesis processes is often complicated. Besides, nanofillers such as carbon quantum dots (CQD), fluorinated carbon black (FCB), fluorinated graphene (FG), and fluorinated graphene quantum dots (FGQD) can be added to modified PI [30,36–38]. Recently, Zhang et al. [39] demonstrated that the addition of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to the PI matrix effectively reduces dielectric loss, with the lowest dielectric loss obtained at a CQD content of 4 % (0.0018 at 1 kHz). However, the nanomaterials such as fluorinated graphene possess unique chemical compositions and nanoscale effects, it is easy to disperse unevenly when the addition amount is high [40]. Apart from this, the scattering effect of graphene oxide on light would significantly reduce photoresist resolution by affecting the effect of ultraviolet light on polymer substrates [41].

Typically, photosensitive polyimides possess photosensitivity, allowing for precise patterning processes such as photolithography and photo-patterning techniques to achieve high-resolution patterns. The photosensitive properties of photosensitive polyimides make them widely applicable in areas such as microelectronics manufacturing, optical devices, and sensors, including applications like fan-out wafer level packaging (FOWLP) for achieving high-precision microstructures and functional components [42,43]. Fan et al. [44] prepared a low-temperature-cured negative photosensitive polyimide viscous solution with excellent dielectric properties, yet its glass transition temperature did not meet the application requirements.

In this work, the introduction of FGQD has demonstrated effective in diminishing the dielectric constant of PI and enhancing the thermal and mechanical properties. Notably, the nanoscale dimensions of FGQD exert negligible influence on the photosensitive characteristics of photosensitive polyimide (PSPI). Consequently, this study employs hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA) as the photosensitive functional group in the synthesis of ester negative photosensitive polyimide. Subsequently, FGQD is introduced into the PSPI matrix with the aim of realizing concurrent low dielectric constant, heightened heat resistance, and enhanced mechanical strength. Furthermore, the addition of FGQD did not affect the lithography performance, where the FGQD/PSPI composite films realized a resolution of 10 μm (L/S). Importantly, the impact of FGQD addition on the comprehensive thermal, mechanical, and electrical properties of PSPI materials is systematically investigated.

Pyromellitic dianhydride (PMDA, 99 %), 4, 4’-oxydianiline (ODA, 99 %) and tetraethylene glycol dimethacrylate (TGD, 99 %) were purchased from TCI (Tokyo, Japan). N-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP, 99 %), pyridine (PY, 99 %) and trifluoroacetic anhydride (TFAA, 99 %) were purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). Hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA, 99 %) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. [1-(4-phenylsulfanylbenzoyl)heptylideneamino] benzoate (OXE01, 99 %) was purchased from Guangzhou Jintu Technology Co., Ltd. Fluorinated graphene quantum dots (FGQD) were prepared referred to our previous reported work [38].

PAE was synthesized according to the previous method [45] 2.62 g PMDA was added to a three-neck flask containing 30 mL of NMP under a nitrogen atmosphere at a temperature of 45 °C. Then 0.31 g of HEMA and 0.18 g of PY were successively added, and mechanical stirred for 2 h until the complete dissolution of the precipitate. 2.04 g of ODA was added under nitrogen at 0 °C, and then stirred for 6 h to complete amidation and obtain a higher molecular weight PI precursor, that is, poly-amine acid(PAA), to improve the quality of subsequent films. The solution containing 5.94 g TFAA in 30 mL NMP was added dropwise to the previous solution to ensure complete isoimidization so as to obtain higher esterification rate and improve photolithography performance. After 2 h, the reaction mixture was removed from the ice bath and the temperature was raised to 50 °C. Dehydration and cyclisation of PAA to polyisoimide (PII) was in the presence of the dehydrating agent TFAA. Subsequently, 7.88 g of HEMA was added, and the reaction proceeded for an additional 12 h. Finally, the resulting solution was added dropwise to 3000 mL of deionized water. 16.69 g of pale yellow product PAE was obtained by extraction and drying, with a yield of 88 %, as shown in Fig. 1b.

21.40 mg of FGQD were introduced into a brown bottle containing 8.35 g of NMP and subjected to 1 h of ultrasonic treatment to disperse it evenly. Subsequently, 428 mg of TGD was employed as a crosslinking agent to enhance mechanical strength, while 21.40 mg of OXE01 served as a photoinitiator to initiate the vinyl polymerization reaction. Additionally, 4.28 g of PAE was introduced, and the mixture was agitated on a shaker for 24 h to achieve a homogeneously dispersed solution of fluorinated graphene quantum dot/polyamide ester (FGQD/PAE). The FGQD/PAE was then uniformly coated onto clean silicon wafers using a spin coater. Finally, heated at a rate of 5 °C/min, maintaining temperatures of 100 °C, 200 °C, 300 °C and 350 °C for one hour each. The thermal imidization process resulted in the formation of FGQD/PSPI composite films[38] denoted as PSPI-0.2, with a FGQD content of 0.2 wt%. Additional films, namely PSPI-0.5 and PSPI-1, were prepared using identical synthesis steps.

The chemical structures inherent in FGQD/PSPI composite films were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker Optik GmbH, Germany) in the attenuated total reflection (ATR) testing mode. The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) samples were prepared by dissolving 7 mg PAE in 1 mL Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO-d6) and tested by a Bruker AVANCE Ⅲ 400 MHz spectrometer (Bruker Optik GmbH, Germany). Elemental analysis of the FGQD was undertaken via X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, ESCALB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher, UK). Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) with tetrahydrofuran (THF) as mobile phase was carried out on a Thermo Fisher 2695 system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc, America) to obtain the molecular weight and polydispersity of PAE. Mechanical properties and the T
g of the composite films were assessed using a dynamic thermomechanical analyzer (DMA, TA Instruments, America) Q800, in which the tensile test was ranging from 2 N min−1 to 18 N and the thermal performance test was ranging from 10 °C/min−1 to 350 °C. The CTE of the composite films were analyzed by thermomechanical analyzer (TMA, TA Instruments, America), in which the test temperature ranging from 200 °C to 300 °C. The size and distribution of FGQD were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI spirit T12, Japan), and the photolithography performance was investigated by a scanning electron microscopy-focused ion Beam (SEM-FIB) Helios 5UX. A comprehensive assessment of the thermal stability was conducted by Discovery SDT650 (TGA, TA Instruments, America). The dielectric properties of the composite material were measured by Agilent 4294 A impedance analyzer. Additionally, the hydrophobic properties of the composite films were characterized by OCA20 Optical Contact Angle Meter (Dataphysics, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NMR spectra and molecular weight analysis of PAE
ce:section.ce:para:
The lithographic performance of PSPI is directly influenced by the grafting rate of HEMA and molecular weight of PAE. To comprehensively assess these factors, an analysis of the HEMA grafting rate and molecular weight of PAE resin was conducted, and the results were presented in Fig. 2. In Fig. 2a, the 1H NMR spectrum of PAE resin revealed characteristic shifts in aromatic protons peaks from 6.93 to 8.50 ppm. Simultaneously, the chemical shift from 5.57 to 6.10 ppm corresponded to the characteristic peaks of HEMA olefin protons. Generally, the esterification rate was calculated according to the number of protons at the double bond position (δ = 5.57–6.10 ppm) and the methyl position (δ = 1.78 ppm) on the PAE. Since the PMDA/ODA ratio approximates 1:1 and each repeating unit contains 10 aromatic protons, the integrated area of aromatic protons is defined as 10.00. Considering that each repeating unit comprises two carboxyl groups, and assuming 100 % esterification, the number of HEMA in each unit is 2, resulting in 4 olefin protons per unit. The integrated area analysis suggested an estimated esterification rate of about 70 % for PAE[46]. The molecular weight of PAE resin was determined through Gel Permeation Chromatography (GPC), as shown in Fig. 2b. Fitting calculations yielded the M
n and M
w values of PAE resin were 7621 g/mol and 10614 g/mol, respectively. This appropriate molecular weight could ensure the requisite strength and toughness of the generated PSPI. Furthermore, the polydispersity of PAE resin was found to be 1.39, indicating a more uniform distribution of molecular weight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS and heat resistance of FGQD
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorine content within FGQD plays a pivotal role in diminishing the dielectric constant of the PSPI composite film, as illustrated in Fig. 3a. It can be seen that FGQD introduces O elements in trace amounts during the preparation process from the XPS spectrum, which may come from the grafting of oxygen-containing groups on the defects of FGQD. The fluorine-to-carbon ratio (F/C) of FGQD was calculated to be 1.02. Furthermore, the thermal resistance of FGQD plays a crucial role in determining the overall heat resistance of the composite film. To analyze this, the thermal stability of FGQD was investigated, as illustrated in Fig. 3b. At approximately 250°C, a sharp decline in the mass of FGQD was observed, likely attributed to the decomposition of oxygen-containing groups on its surface. A further significant mass reduction was noted at 500°C, which may be due to the loss of fluorine atoms from the FGQD surface.

The extent of imidization would directly influence the comprehensive properties of PSPI. To evaluate the chemical structure of PSPI composite films, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed, as depicted in Fig. 4. The FTIR spectra for Pure-PSPI, PSPI-0.2, PSPI-0.5, and PSPI-1 composite films revealed four distinct characteristic peaks, including the asymmetric stretching absorption peak of the CO double bond at 1780 cm−1, the symmetrical stretching characteristic peak of the CO double bond at 1720 cm−1, the stretching absorption peak of the C-N bond at 1380 cm−1, and the bending characteristic peak of the CO double bond at 725 cm−1. Furthermore, the FTIR spectra indicated the absence of the asymmetric vibration characteristic peak of the acetyl group in hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA) at 1410 cm−1 with the conversion of polyamide ester (PAE) resin to PSPI film. The phenomenon implied the pyrolysis of HEMA on the main skeleton of the PAE polymer during thermal imidization, indicating the complete transformation of PAE into PSPI during high-temperature dehydration. Notably, the introduction of FGQD did not exert any discernible impact on the synthesis of PSPI under these conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lithography process and performance evaluation of FGQD/PSPI
ce:section.ce:para: The uniform spreading of fluorinated graphene quantum dots/polyamide ester composite solution on clean silicon wafer was realized by spin coating. The subsequent patterning process involved the following steps: the spin-coated wafer substrate underwent soft-baking on a hot plate at 100 °C for 5 min to eliminate a portion of the organic solvent, resulting in a spin-coated film with an approximate thickness of 5 μm. Subsequently, a line pattern mask featuring a width ratio of 1:1 between exposed and unexposed segments within a 10 μm span was employed, using the spin-coated film as a reference. The exposure dose applied was 500 mJ/cm2, followed by placing the wafers on a hot plate and baking at 100 °C for 5 min. Then, the wafer is lithographically exposed and developed in cyclopentanone to form a linear pattern. Finally, the FGQD/PSPI composite film was obtained by thermal amination at 100 °C, 200 °C, 300 °C, and 350 °C for one hour each. Cross-sectional observation of the FGQD/PSPI films was conducted using focused ion beam (FIB) sectioning, and scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as depicted in Fig. 5. The clear and straight lines within the pattern were observed, and the line edge roughness was low, achieving a resolution of approximately 10 μm. The line aspect ratio was close to 1:1, which can meet the requirements of fine pattern transfer of integrated circuits in the microelectronics industry. Additionally, the introduction of FGQD has no significant effect on the photolithography performance of PSPI.

Electrical properties serve as crucial metrics for assessing the viability of polymers in microelectronics. Accordingly, the dielectric properties of the composite films were meticulously examined. The PI films obtained were subjected to a baking process in an oven at 100 °C for a minimum duration of 4 h. Subsequently, the dielectric constant and dielectric loss of the films were measured using a vector network analyzer. For calibration purposes, air was utilized as the reference sample[38]. The test were repeated at least 5 times distinct points, and the results were averaged to ensure accuracy, as shown in Fig. 6 [43]. It is evident that the dielectric constant of PSPI composite films followed a trend of initial increase followed by a subsequent decrease upon the introduction of FGQD. Comparative analysis with Pure-PSPI (3.8 @ 1 MHz) revealed a notable decline in permittivity for PSPI-0.2, reaching 3.0 with a 0.2 wt% addition of FGQD. This decline can be attributed to the abundance of fluorine atoms on the surface of FGQD, effectively diminishing the polarizability of the composite film and consequently lowering its dielectric constant [47]. Conversely, for PSPI-0.5, the dielectric constant increased from 3.0 to 3.5 as the FGQD addition reached 0.5 wt%. PSPI-1 continued to exhibit an increasing trend in dielectric constant with escalating FGQD content. This behavior might be attributed to the heightened FGQD content resulted in increased substantial specific surface area, reinforcing interface polarization between FGQD and PSPI. This phenomenon was conducive to electron transport, thereby enhanced the dielectric constant of the composite films. Moreover, the introduction of FGQD exerted negligible influenced on the dielectric loss of the composite film, as illustrated in Fig. 6b. In comparison to Pure-PSPI (0.017 @ 1 MHz), there was no significant change of PSPI-0.2 in dielectric loss with FGQD introduction. However, the continued addition of FGQD increased dielectric loss of the PI composite film. Specifically, when the FGQD addition reached 1 wt%, the dielectric loss experienced a slight elevation to 0.022. This effect might be explained by the extensive specific surface area of FGQD, augmenting interfacial polarization with PSPI and thereby facilitating electron transport[48], and ultimately contributed to the increased dielectric loss of the PSPI composite film.

The mechanical properties of PSPI are of paramount importance in microelectronics applications. Consequently, the cured film was made into a specimens with a width of 3 mm by laser cutting, as shown in Fig. 7a–b. The mechanical properties of PSPI composite films were tested using DMA, and the results were presented in Fig. 7c–f. The incorporation of FGQD exhibited a significant enhancement in the mechanical properties of PSPI, and displayed a trend of initial increase and subsequent decrease. The corresponding data for the mechanical properties of FGQD-PSPI were summarized in Table 1. As for Pure-PSPI, the tensile strength, elongation at break, and Young's modulus were 84.5 MPa, 5.24 %, and 2.68 GPa, respectively. Upon increasing the FGQD content to 0.5 wt%, the tensile strength of PSPI-0.5 increased to 113.8 MPa, the elongation at break increased to 17.5 %, and Young's modulus increased to 3.02 GPa. This enhancement was attributed to the substantial specific surface area of FGQD, uniform size, and improved dispersion within the PSPI matrix, resulting in a more compact arrangement of PSPI molecules. Furthermore, the presence of numerous fluorine atoms and a small quantity of oxygen-containing groups on the FGQD surface facilitated the formation of hydrogen bonds with PSPI, augmenting the mechanical properties of PSPI composite films. The introduction of FGQD enhanced the interaction with PSPI, improved the rigidity of the composite film and subsequently increased the tensile strength and Young's modulus[5]. Compared to Pure-PSPI, the tensile strength, elongation at break, and Young's modulus of PSPI-0.5 increased by 34.7 %, 234 %, and 12.7 %, respectively. However, with a continued increase in FGQD content, the tensile strength, elongation at break, and Young's modulus of the composite films were decreased. For PSPI-1, the tensile strength, elongation at break, and Young's modulus were 101.2 MPa, 9.4 %, and 2.79 GPa, respectively. This decrease may be due to excessive FGQD content led to uneven distribution within the PSPI matrix. Nonetheless, in contrast to Pure-PSPI, the tensile strength, elongation at break, and Young's modulus of PSPI-1 were slightly higher, suggesting that the introduction of FGQD effectively enhanced the mechanical properties of the composite films and facilitated stress transfer within them.

To assess the thermal performance of PSPI composite films, the T 5 %, T g, and CTE were investigated using TGA, DMA, and TMA, as illustrated in Fig. 8. It was evident that the introduction of FGQD marginally increased T g values of PSPI composite films compared to that of Pure-PSPI (T g=396 °C). Additionally, the T g of PSPI composite films exhibited a gradual rise with increasing FGQD addition. Specifically, the T g of PSPI-0.2, PSPI-0.5, and PSPI-1 increased to 412 °C, 419 °C, and 420 °C, respectively. This observed enhancement was likely attributable to the formation of robust interactions between FGQD and PSPI molecules. Subsequently, the T 5 % of the PI composite films exhibited no significant variation with the introduction of FGQD, while PSPI-0.2, PSPI-0.5, and PSPI-1 showed T 5 % values as 406 °C, 398 °C, and 402 °C, respectively (Fig. 8c–d). Moreover, the low molecular weight of polyamide ester (PAE) led to an insignificant improvement in heat resistance[45]. Finally, as shown in Fig. 8e, the CTE curves of films were studied, and the CTE values of the films were obtained through software calculation and analysis. The test of CTE was to compare the matching degree with the copper substrate. If the CTE does not match, it would cause warping. In Fig. 8e, perhaps due to the small volume of the introduced FGQD, the trends of the four curves were basically consistent, and the CTE values were calculated (Fig. 8f). The introduction of FGQD increased the CTE of PSPI-0.2, PSPI-0.5, and PSPI-1, which was 45.65 ppm/°C, 45.91 ppm/°C, and 45.00 ppm/°C, respectively, while the CTE valve was 43.43 ppm/°C for Pure-PSPI. This association may be attributed to that the introduction of FGQD caused defects within the PSPI composite films, resulting in a slight increase in their CTEs.

Hydrophobicity is a critical requirement for the reliability of electronic components. Therefore, the water contact angle of the films was systematically characterized. Fig. 9 illustrated a pronounced increase in the hydrophobicity of the films with the addition of FGQD. In comparison to Pure-PSPI (80.8°), the water contact angle of PSPI-0.2 was 87.2° with a 0.2 wt% addition of FGQD. Furthermore, the water contact angle of PSPI-1 reached 90.6° when the FGQD addition reached 1 wt%. This enhancement resulted from the substantial introduction of fluorine atoms on the surface of FGQD. The low polarity of the carbon-fluorine (C-F) bond hindered water molecules from infiltrating the surface of fluorine-containing substances characterized by low surface tension, thereby achieving a hydrophobic effect. This hydrophobic effect was further supported by the concurrent decrease in the dielectric constant of the composite film, emphasizing the significance of improved hydrophobicity.

In this study, low dielectric constant composite films incorporating FGQD-PSPI were synthesized through an in-situ polymerization process. The incorporation of FGQD resulted in increased tensile strength (113.8 MPa), elongation at break (17.5 %), and Young's modulus (3.02 GPa), representing increments of 34.7 %, 234 %, and 19.4 %, respectively. Furthermore, the dielectric constant of the composite film decreased from 3.8 to 3.0 at 1 MHz when added 0.2 wt% FGQD. Apart from this, it showed excellent heat resistance with T 5 % and T g values of 406 °C and 412 °C, respectively, and the hydrophobicity also exhibited improvement. The exceptional comprehensive performance may be attributed to the effective dispersion of FGQD within the PSPI matrix, where the large specific surface area enhances contact with the polyimide matrix, reinforcing interfacial interactions. The presence of fluorine atoms on the FGQD surface effectively reduces the polarization rate of the composite film, thereby enhancing its dielectric properties. Consequently, FGQD/PI prepared in this work presented potential applications in the future development of advanced packaging and the microelectronics industry.

Hangqian Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Shuye Zhang: Funding acquisition. Xialei Lv: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Jinhui Li: Methodology, Conceptualization. Jialin Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Rong Sun: Funding acquisition. Guoping Zhang: Funding acquisition. Fangfang Niu: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61904191, 62174170, 62304141 and 52375327), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2022B1515120037 and 2023A1515010766), Key R&D Project of Guangdong Province (2020B010180001), Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory (2014B030301014) and Heilongjiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. YQ2022E024).
